questions.question: What is the maximum length of a file name in Unix?
options: 256 characters
512 characters
1024 characters
Unlimited
questions.explanation: In Unix, the maximum length of a file name is 256 characters.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the '/' in Unix file names?
options: To specify the owner of a file
To specify the group of a file
To indicate the root directory
To denote a hidden file
questions.explanation: The '/' in Unix file names indicates the root directory.

questions.question: Can a Unix file name contain special characters?
options: Yes, but only letters and numbers
Yes, but only spaces and punctuation
No, only letters and numbers
Yes, but only specific special characters
questions.explanation: Yes, Unix file names can contain special characters, but they must be enclosed in quotes.

questions.question: Can a Unix file name be a directory?
options: No, but it can be a subdirectory
Yes, but it must end with a dot
Yes, but it must be enclosed in quotes
No, it must start with a dot
questions.explanation: Yes, a Unix file name can be a directory, but it must be enclosed in quotes when used in a command.

questions.question: How are file names in Unix case-sensitive?
options: Case-insensitive
Case-insensitive, but with some exceptions
Case-sensitive, but only for file extensions
Case-sensitive, like in Windows
questions.explanation: Unix file names are case-sensitive, meaning that 'File' and 'file' are considered different names.
